edges.description: Excess BMP signaling disrupts normal joint-space formation during skeletal development.
Failed joint separation produces proximal interphalangeal synostosis.
Abnormal cavitation and ossification extend to carpal and tarsal joints.
Abnormal skeletal joint separation can include humeroradial fusion.
Abnormal joint morphogenesis can involve cervical spine joint fusion.
Progressive synostosis limits joint range of motion and produces stiffness.
Middle-ear ossicular fixation in the synostosis spectrum produces conductive hearing impairment.
Altered BMP signaling in limb development contributes to shortened digits.
Altered BMP signaling in limb development contributes to broad thumbs.

nodes.description: Normal joint formation requires interzone specification and subsequent cavitation. Excess BMP signaling, due to loss of noggin antagonism, disrupts this process, leading to failure of joint space formation and progressive synostosis.

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous mutations in the gene encoding noggin affect human joint morphogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Nog-/- mice die at birth from multiple defects that include bony fusion of the appendicular skeleton.

evidence.reference_title: Human disease-causing NOG missense mutations: effects on noggin secretion, dimer formation, and bone morphogenetic protein binding.
evidence.snippet: noggin has both species-specific and joint-specific dosage-dependent roles during joint formation.

nodes.description: NOG mutations reduce noggin's ability to antagonize BMPs (particularly BMP2, BMP4, and GDF5), leading to excessive BMP signaling in developing joints. This causes progressive ossification and fusion of joints that would normally remain separate. GDF5 mutations cause gain-of-function changes that similarly enhance BMP pathway activity in joint development.

evidence.reference_title: Human disease-causing NOG missense mutations: effects on noggin secretion, dimer formation, and bone morphogenetic protein binding.
evidence.snippet: The SYNS1 mutation abolished, and the SYM1 mutations reduced, the secretion of functional noggin dimers in transiently transfected COS-7 cells.

evidence.reference_title: New insights into the molecular mechanism of multiple synostoses syndrome (SYNS): mutation within the GDF5 knuckle epitope causes noggin-resistance.
evidence.snippet: this gain of function is caused by strongly reduced affinity of GDF5-S94N to the BMP/GDF antagonist noggin and the consequential lack of noggin inhibition.

nodes.description: Short digits are part of the multiple synostoses limb phenotype.
Broad thumbs are reported in the symphalangism-brachydactyly MSS spectrum.
Progressive fusion of carpal (wrist) and tarsal (ankle) bones.

evidence.reference_title: GDF5 is a second locus for multiple-synostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Multiple-synostosis syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by progressive symphalangism, carpal/tarsal fusions, deafness, and mild facial dysmorphism.

evidence.reference_title: New insights into the molecular mechanism of multiple synostoses syndrome (SYNS): mutation within the GDF5 knuckle epitope causes noggin-resistance.
evidence.snippet: SYNS is characterized by progressive symphalangism, carpal/tarsal fusions, deafness and mild facial dysmorphism.

nodes.description: Conductive hearing loss resulting from stapes fixation or fusion of the ossicular chain in the middle ear.

evidence.reference_title: GDF5 is a second locus for multiple-synostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Multiple-synostosis syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by progressive symphalangism, carpal/tarsal fusions, deafness, and mild facial dysmorphism.
HP:0000405 | Conductive hearing impairment | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Fusion of the humerus and radius at the elbow, part of the broader multiple synostoses pattern.

evidence.snippet: Multiple synostoses syndrome (MSS) is a rare developmental bone disorder characterized by proximal symphalangism of the fingers and/or toes often associated with fusion of carpal and tarsal, humeroradial, and cervical spine joints.

nodes.description: Progressive synostosis produces stiff joints and limited range of motion.
Fusion of proximal interphalangeal joints, causing inability to flex the fingers. One of the earliest and most consistent findings.

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous mutations in the gene encoding noggin affect human joint morphogenesis.
evidence.snippet: We have identified five dominant human NOG mutations in unrelated families segregating proximal symphalangism (SYM1; OMIM 185800)

evidence.reference_title: GDF5 is a second locus for multiple-synostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Multiple-synostosis syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by progressive symphalangism, carpal/tarsal fusions, deafness, and mild facial dysmorphism.

nodes.description: Fusion of vertebrae, particularly cervical, limiting neck mobility.

evidence.snippet: Multiple synostoses syndrome (MSS) is a rare developmental bone disorder characterized by proximal symphalangism of the fingers and/or toes often associated with fusion of carpal and tarsal, humeroradial, and cervical spine joints.
